bar.name: Thunderbolt
bar.address: 1263 W Temple St, Los Angeles, CA 90026
bar.happyHour: Mon-Thu 5-7pm specials; serious cocktail list daily
bar.vibe: Southern-inspired cocktail bar, open patio, light pink highlights, dark green walls, orange stools, brown leather upholstery, Technics 1200 turntables through a vintage 1985 Macintosh amplifier, centrifuge-made drinks
barFacts: #10 on North America's 50 Best Bars list — named 'Best Bar in the West' three years running
Owner-bartender Mike Capoferri uses centrifuges and high-pressure nitrous to make cocktails
25+ Madeira wines on the list, oldest from the 1960s — one of the deepest Madeira programs in LA
Ceiling completely soundproofed with acoustic panelling; audio routed through vintage 1985 Macintosh amplifier
Winner of the Ketel One Sustainable Bar Award 2025
Named at the intersection of three neighborhoods: Historic Filipinotown, Echo Park, and DTLA

stops.label: Filipino Christian Church & HiFi Western Gateway

pois.name: Unidad Park & Gintong Kasaysayan Mural
designations: Named one of '10 Monumental Murals of LA' and '20 Iconic Murals of LA' by LA Weekly
facts: Gintong Kasaysayan, Gintong Pamana ('A Glorious History, A Golden Legacy') — 145 ft × 30 ft, the largest Filipino American mural in the United States
Painted by Eliseo Art Silva in 1995, commissioned by the Social and Public Art Resource Center (SPARC)
Summarizes 5,000 years of Filipino and Filipino American history — first mural to honor Larry Itliong and the 1965 Delano Grape Strike
Depicts Jose Rizal, Carlos Bulosan, Lapu-Lapu, Larry Itliong, Philip Vera Cruz, and others — interpretive sign in front identifies each figure
The mural's presence helped inspire the city to designate Historic Filipinotown in 2002
Park includes a dap-ay — a circular gathering place from Cordillera mountain villages in Northern Luzon, Philippines — believed to be the first outside the Philippines
Entrance walkway inspired by the yo-yo, which was popularized by Filipino immigrant Pedro Flores
Park design references the Rice Terraces of the Philippine Cordilleras (UNESCO World Heritage Site)
Before it was Unidad Park, the site was the Candy Chuateco Community Garden — SIPA broke ground in 1993
18 raised wooden garden beds built and installed by residents in 2012
LA Times called it 'the most significant Filipino mural in the country'
Laid the cultural foundation for the first Larry Itliong Day celebration in Carson, CA (2010), later adopted statewide in 2015

features.detail: 145-ft × 30-ft mural spanning the entire western edge of the park

features.text: Interpretive panel identifying depicted figures — Jose Rizal, Lapu-Lapu, Larry Itliong, Philip Vera Cruz, Carlos Bulosan, and more

features.detail: Dap-ay — circular stone gathering place
Entrance walkway design inspired by the knotted yo-yo
Playground, barbecue grills, covered tent

features.element: Approximate length of mural in feet

features.detail: Historical figures painted in mural — countable from interpretive sign

connections.link: Same artist designed both the mural (1995) and the Eastern Gateway arch (2022)
Both honor Itliong's role in the Delano Grape Strike
The church anchored the Filipino community here; the mural gave it a visible identity 45 years later
Both represent Filipino community-building transplanted to LA — sacred and secular

pois.name: Filipino Christian Church
facts: Oldest Filipino Protestant church in the United States — founded in 1928 as the Filipino Christian Fellowship
Founded by young Filipino men enrolled at California Christian College who faced discriminatory practices restricting where they could live, work, and worship
Reorganized as a formal church in 1933
Forced to move repeatedly due to city redevelopment until 1950, when it acquired the former Union Avenue M.E. Church in the Temple-Beverly corridor
The church's move to this corridor catalyzed other Filipino organizations relocating here — directly creating what became Historic Filipinotown
Only historic cultural monument designated by the City of Los Angeles with Filipino origins
German Gothic Revival and Craftsman architecture
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2019 by the National Park Service
The broader Filipino community in LA dates to the early 1900s — restrictive housing laws prevented land ownership until the 1950s

features.detail: German Gothic Revival and Craftsman style church building
Former Union Avenue M.E. Church — original structure predates Filipino congregation

connections.link: Church's 1950 relocation to Temple-Beverly corridor directly created the neighborhood's Filipino identity
Church anchored community for 45 years before the mural gave it visible cultural expression
Filipinos couldn't own land until 1950s — forced from Downtown's Little Manila to Temple-Beverly corridor

pois.name: HiFi Western Gateway
designations: City of Los Angeles commissioned landmark
facts: Unveiled in 2002 by then-City Councilmember Eric Garcetti — marks the western entrance to Historic Filipinotown
Facade designed with vine and floral motifs known as 'callado' embroidery — traditional patterns from the Maria Clara (women's formal dress) and Barong Tagalog (men's formal shirt)
Part of the 30-year campaign to get the city to designate Historic Filipinotown in 2002
Historic Filipinotown was the first Filipino community in America to receive a named area with distinct geographic boundaries
Complements the Eastern Gateway (Talang Gabay) installed 20 years later in 2022

features.detail: Gateway facade with callado embroidery motifs — vine and floral patterns
Callado patterns reference both Maria Clara dress and Barong Tagalog shirt

connections.link: 20-year bookends — western installed at designation, eastern completed two decades later
Garcetti unveiled western gateway as councilmember; eastern gateway completed during his mayoralty
Western gateway references clothing tradition; eastern references spiritual/cultural symbols — two different dimensions of Filipino identity

pois.name: Screen Novelties / Neon Giraffe Sign
facts: Stop-motion animation studio formed in 2003 by Mark Caballero, Seamus Walsh, and Christopher Finnegan
14-foot neon giraffe sign on the building — originally said 'DELI' at the bottom, now says 'Hi-Fi' (abbreviation for Historic Filipinotown)
The giraffe sign includes an X-ray-like view of the giraffe's neck in gold neon
Studio partners restored the vintage sign when they moved into the brick building
Among the first stop-motion artists to adopt an entirely digital capture system — integral in launching both Robot Chicken and Moral Orel for Adult Swim
Atlas Obscura describes it as a 'puppet-lover's paradise'
Building is a brick structure in Historic Filipinotown section of Echo Park

features.text: 14-foot neon giraffe with X-ray neck view and 'Hi-Fi' text

features.detail: Brick building
Originally a deli sign — animation studio kept the giraffe and changed 'DELI' to 'Hi-Fi'
Interior filled with puppets and stop-motion sets — visible through windows

connections.link: Both are structures that got famous second lives — deli became animation studio, grocery became film location
~100m apart on Temple St — closest POI to the bar
Both film production in HiFi — silent era and modern animation, same neighborhood 100 years apart

pois.name: Talang Gabay — HiFi Eastern Gateway
designations: City of Los Angeles commissioned landmark
facts: Formally named 'Talang Gabay — Our Guiding Star' — name drawn from the Jose Rizal Monument in Manila, which was called 'Motto Stella' (Latin: Guiding Star)
Spans 82 feet across Beverly Boulevard, stands 30 feet high — the largest Filipino cultural arch in the United States
Designed by Eliseo Art Silva (same artist as the Gintong Kasaysayan mural) with Celestino Geronimo Jr.
Incorporates: parol (star-shaped Christmas lantern), gumamela flower (hibiscus, tribute to COVID-19 frontline workers), Sarimanok (mythical bird symbolizing good fortune)
Peak of the arch replicates precolonial Filipino palaces such as the Torogan, and boat masts with bird motifs
Two ends of the arch represent the Naga — a mythological sea creature that provides safe passage to sailors
The face on the Philippine Sun restores the original flag design launched June 12, 1898
Cost approximately $587,000 — project took 20 years from concept to completion
Installed April 2022, formally unveiled May 2022
Eric Garcetti championed the project as city councilmember; it was completed during his term as mayor

features.detail: 82-ft wide × 30-ft tall steel arch spanning Beverly Blvd
Parol (star-shaped lantern) incorporated into arch design
Gumamela (hibiscus) flower element
Sarimanok mythical bird figure
Naga sea creature figures at arch ends
Torogan-inspired peak with boat mast and bird motifs
Philippine Sun face — original 1898 flag design

connections.link: Same artist — mural (1995) and gateway (2022), 27 years of Filipino cultural expression in one neighborhood
20-year bookends marking the neighborhood's entrances
National hero appears in both the arch's naming and the mural's imagery

pois.name: Bob's Market / Toretto's Market
facts: Built in 1913 — architect George F. Colterison designed it for Ella J. McMillen as two separate shops: a market and a tailor
Mission Revival and Oriental architectural elements on a one-story false-front frame structure
One of the earliest tenants, tailor Levon Melkonian, fled the Armenian Genocide in 1914 and set up shop in the 1253 Bellevue half
Two shops combined in 1934 when the grocery expanded
Named 'Bob's Market' when the Nimura family bought it in 1965
Designated HCM #215 on June 6, 1979 for 'many decades of service as a grocery store with little alteration to the main building'
Featured as Toretto's Market & Cafe in The Fast and the Furious (2001) — Vin Diesel's character's family store
The garage in the movie is fictional but the rest of the building is real

features.detail: One-story false-front frame structure with Mission Revival and Oriental elements
Corner store configuration — market side and former tailor side visible
Fans of The Fast and the Furious frequently visit to photograph the building

connections.link: Both Fast & Furious filming locations within 200m of each other in Angelino Heights
Immigrant communities finding refuge in the same neighborhood across decades
Both founded in the same year — a grocery store and a film studio, same neighborhood

pois.name: Carroll Avenue Historic District
facts: Finest collection of Victorian domestic architecture remaining in Los Angeles — Queen Anne and Eastlake styles from the late 19th century
Angelino Heights subdivided in 1886 by William Stilson and Everett Hall during the LA land boom
Banking recession of 1888 halted construction — accidentally preserving the Victorian character
1329 Carroll Ave: The 'Charmed' house (Halliwell Manor exterior) — the Innes House, built for city councilman and real estate developer Daniel Innes in the late 1880s
1345 Carroll Ave: The Sanders House — featured in the final scene of Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' video
Foy House: Oldest home on Carroll Ave, built 1872 in Italianate style — moved twice. Former resident Mary Foy was LA's chief librarian and a leader in the women's suffragette movement
Over 30 Historic-Cultural Monuments in the broader Angelino Heights neighborhood
1300 block listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1976

features.detail: Queen Anne facades with turrets, gingerbread trim, ornate porches
Eastlake style wooden detailing
Innes House / Charmed House at 1329 — late 1880s construction
Sanders House / Thriller House at 1345
Foy House is the oldest structure — 1872 Italianate, moved twice to its current location

connections.link: The crash that killed the boom is the reason the Victorians survived — too broke to demolish and rebuild
Three iconic film/TV locations within 400m of each other in Angelino Heights
Victorian 1880s and Modernist 1941 — two architectural eras 200m apart

pois.name: Fast & Furious House (Toretto's House)
facts: Built in 1906 — four-bedroom house with no notable history before the films
Featured as Dominic Toretto's house (fictional address 1327) in six Fast and Furious films: The Fast and the Furious, Fast & Furious, Fast & Furious 6, Furious 7, F9, and Fast X
The garage in the movie is fictional — only the house exterior is real
Private residence — fans frequently visit to photograph it
Real address is 722 E Kensington Rd; movie address is 1327

features.detail: 1906 residential house — Craftsman-era construction
Fans and tourists frequently photographing the house
No garage actually exists — the movie garage was built on a set

connections.link: Both Fast & Furious locations within 200m — house and family store
Angelino Heights as Hollywood's backlot — three franchises from one neighborhood

pois.name: Occidental Studios
facts: Founded in 1913 by Hobart Bosworth — 112 years of continuous operation as a film studio
Originally just office bungalows and an open-air stage with no roof or lighting for shooting silent movies
Mary Pickford, Douglas Fairbanks, Cecil B. DeMille, and D.W. Griffith all worked here
'The Squaw Man' (1914) — the first feature-length picture made in Hollywood — was filmed at Occidental Studios
3-acre gated campus with 31,489 sq ft of stage space
Longest continuously operating studio lot in Los Angeles
Listed for sale at $45 million in 2025 — future use uncertain

features.detail: Gated studio campus — walls and gates visible from street
3-acre film campus hidden in a residential neighborhood

connections.link: Both founded the same year — filmmaking and groceries, same neighborhood
Film production in HiFi across 110 years — silent era to Robot Chicken
DeMille worked here before Hollywood was 'Hollywood'

pois.name: Neutra/Maxwell House
facts: 1,200-sq-ft Mid-Century modern house designed by Richard Neutra in 1941
Built for musicians Charles and Sybil Maxwell — she a violinist, he a composer — who wanted 'acoustically suitable living quarters' on a budget of $6,750
Her practice room is farthest from the composer's study — the house was designed around sound separation
Neutra first experimented here with peaked roofs and triangular glazed gable openings — this design element profoundly influenced American suburban architecture in the 1950s and 60s
Originally at 475 N Bowling Green Way in Brentwood — relocated to Angelino Heights in 2008 by Barbara Behm
Integration of sloping and flat roofs with a glass triangle set into redwood siding

features.detail: Mid-Century modern house with peaked roof and triangular glazed gable
Integration of sloping and flat roofs
House was physically moved from Brentwood to Angelino Heights — 15+ miles

connections.link: Two architectural eras 200m apart — Victorian gingerbread and Mid-Century glass
Angelino Heights as a neighborhood that collects displaced architecture

pois.name: Filipino Cultural Lamp Posts
designations: City of Los Angeles / Metro commissioned
facts: 54 lamp posts adorned with Filipino cultural medallions by artist Roel Punzalan
Placed at 17 different intersections throughout the neighborhood
Medallions encode three Filipino values: 'kapwa' (shared humanity/togetherness), 'lakbay' (journey), and 'kapayapaan' (peace)
Commissioned by the City of Los Angeles and Metro
Unveiled by Councilmember Mitch O'Farrell during Filipino American History Month, October 2016
Serve dual purpose: cultural education and pedestrian safety

features.detail: Filipino cultural medallions on lamp posts

features.text: Kapwa / Lakbay / Kapayapaan — three values encoded in the medallion designs

connections.link: Both encode Filipino values in physical infrastructure — medallions on streets, gathering circle in park
Sequential layering of Filipino cultural markers in the built environment

pois.name: Larry Itliong Utility Box Mural
facts: Painted utility box depicting Filipino American labor leader Larry Itliong
Itliong organized Filipino farmworkers to strike Delano grape growers on September 8, 1965 — eight days before Cesar Chavez and the NFWA joined
The Delano Grape Strike (1965-1970) became the defining moment of the American farmworker movement
Itliong's role was largely erased from public memory for decades — Chavez received most of the credit
First Larry Itliong Day celebrated in Carson, CA in 2010; adopted as a statewide California observance in 2015
Located near the Gintong Kasaysayan mural, which was the first public art to honor Itliong

features.detail: Painted portrait of Larry Itliong on utility box
Easy to walk past — a labor hero memorialized on a utility box rather than a monument

connections.link: Itliong depicted in both the utility box and the large mural — first public art to honor him
Pattern of Filipino contributions being made invisible — in labor history and in urban geography

pois.name: Temple Street (the street itself)
facts: Named for John Temple (1796-1866), a Massachusetts tradesman who arrived in El Pueblo de los Angeles c. 1828
Temple converted to Catholicism and took the Spanish name 'Juan' after moving to Mexican California
He opened the city's first general store on what became Temple Block — one of the most important retail buildings in early Los Angeles
The street was not named for a house of worship — a common misconception
Temple Street runs from Figueroa through Historic Filipinotown — the bar sits on a street named for a man who arrived when LA was still part of Mexico

features.text: Temple St street signs visible at intersections

connections.link: Same street, 100 years apart — Massachusetts merchant under Mexican rule, Filipino immigrants under American rule
Commerce on this corridor for nearly 200 years

stop1_unidadParkMural.notes: The mural is a spotter goldmine — interpretive sign lists countable figures. '5,000 years of history in 145 feet' is a reframe. The yo-yo walkway (Pedro Flores invented it) is a connector to Filipino immigrant history. Dap-ay (first outside Philippines) is a narrative-riddle. Larry Itliong's erased role in the Grape Strike is a reframe — he struck 8 days before Chavez. The three Filipino values on the lampposts (kapwa/lakbay/kapayapaan) are cipher-lite material. Mural figures as an elimination clue: 'Which of these is NOT depicted in the mural?'

stop2_filipinoChristianChurch.notes: The church-as-neighborhood-creator is a perfect reframe — a congregation forced to move repeatedly by redevelopment, and when it finally settled, it pulled an entire community into existence. The Western Gateway's callado embroidery references clothing (Maria Clara / Barong Tagalog) — spotter for the pattern. '30-year campaign' for HiFi designation is a narrative-riddle. 'First Filipino community in America with distinct geographic boundaries' is a connector to the broader Filipino diaspora. German Gothic Revival + Craftsman architecture on a Filipino church is an inherent reframe.

stop3_screenNoveltiesBlock.notes: The neon giraffe (14 ft, X-ray neck, changed from DELI to Hi-Fi) is the richest spotter near the bar. Bob's Market reframe: 'A 1913 grocery store designed for a widow, where an Armenian Genocide survivor set up a tailor shop, now famous as Vin Diesel's family store.' Carroll Avenue sequence: Foy House (1872) → Victorian boom (1886) → recession freezes time (1888) → Thriller (1983) → Charmed (1998). The Fast & Furious house has a great cipher-lite: real address 722 vs. movie address 1327. Robot Chicken + silent films in the same neighborhood 100 years apart is a connector. Temple Street named for a man, not a building — reframe for the bar's own address.

overpassBuildingStock.note: Angelino Heights / HiFi building stock is overwhelmingly pre-war. Within 500m radius, 90% of dated buildings were constructed before 1940. The 1886 land boom and subsequent 1888 crash froze development, preserving Victorian-era structures.

neighborhoodContext.hifiDesignation: 2002 — first Filipino community in America with named area and distinct geographic boundaries, after a 30-year campaign
neighborhoodContext.filipinoPopulation: Greater LA is home to the largest Filipino American population in the US — over 600,000 people
neighborhoodContext.littleManila: A Filipino community called Little Manila flourished in Downtown LA (now Little Tokyo) from the 1920s-1940s — completely destroyed by 1950s redevelopment, pushing the community to the Temple-Beverly corridor
neighborhoodContext.timeOutRanking: Historic Filipinotown named 5th coolest neighborhood in the world by Time Out (2019)
neighborhoodContext.hifiTours: Pilipino Workers Center runs Mobile HiFi tours (since 2009) and Jeepney tours in a converted 1944 Willys Jeep
